primitives.name: Set Definition (ZFC)
primitives.formalStatement: A set is a well-defined collection of distinct objects (elements). Membership is denoted x in S. Two sets are equal iff they have exactly the same elements (Axiom of Extensionality). Sets are the foundational objects of mathematics under ZFC.
primitives.computationalForm: Define sets by listing: S = {1, 2, 3} or by comprehension: S = {x : P(x)}. Test membership: x in S. Key operations: union, intersection, complement. Sets can contain other sets.

compositionRules.yields: Set builder notation: {x in S : P(x)} uses logical predicates to define sets
compositionRules.example: Even numbers: {x in Z : exists k in Z, x = 2k}

applicabilityPatterns: Define a collection of mathematical objects
Establish the foundational objects for building mathematical structures
Work with membership, inclusion, and equality of collections

primitives.name: Empty Set
primitives.formalStatement: The empty set {} (also written as emptyset) is the unique set containing no elements. For all x: x not in {}. The empty set exists (Axiom of Empty Set) and is a subset of every set.
primitives.computationalForm: emptyset = {}. |emptyset| = 0. For any set S: emptyset subset S. emptyset union S = S. emptyset intersection S = emptyset.

compositionRules.yields: Basis for inductive set construction: {}, {{}}, {{},{{}}}, ...
conditions: Constructing ordinals or natural numbers from sets
compositionRules.example: Von Neumann ordinals: 0 = {}, 1 = {{}}, 2 = {{}, {{}}}

applicabilityPatterns: Represent the absence of elements in a collection
Serve as the base case for inductive set constructions
Act as the identity element for set union

primitives.name: Set Union
primitives.formalStatement: The union of sets A and B is A union B = {x : x in A or x in B}. For a family of sets {A_i}: union A_i = {x : exists i, x in A_i}. Union is commutative, associative, and distributes over intersection.
primitives.computationalForm: A union B: include all elements from both sets. |A union B| = |A| + |B| - |A intersection B| (inclusion-exclusion). Iterate for multiple sets.

dependencies.description: Union is an operation on sets

compositionRules.yields: Boolean algebra of sets: union and intersection with complement form a complete Boolean algebra
compositionRules.example: A union (B intersection C) = (A union B) intersection (A union C) (distributivity)

applicabilityPatterns: Combine elements from multiple collections
Count elements using inclusion-exclusion
Build larger sets from smaller ones

primitives.name: Set Intersection
primitives.formalStatement: The intersection of sets A and B is A intersection B = {x : x in A and x in B}. For a family: intersection A_i = {x : for all i, x in A_i}. Intersection is commutative, associative, and distributes over union.
primitives.computationalForm: A intersection B: include only elements common to both. |A intersection B| = |A| + |B| - |A union B|. Disjoint sets: A intersection B = emptyset.

dependencies.description: Intersection is an operation on sets

compositionRules.yields: Set algebra with distributive laws: A inter (B union C) = (A inter B) union (A inter C)

applicabilityPatterns: Find elements common to multiple collections
Test if two sets are disjoint
Filter a collection by a criterion

primitives.name: Set Complement
primitives.formalStatement: The complement of A relative to universal set U is A^c = U \ A = {x in U : x not in A}. The set difference A \ B = {x in A : x not in B}. Complement satisfies: (A^c)^c = A, A union A^c = U, A inter A^c = emptyset.
primitives.computationalForm: A^c = U - A. |A^c| = |U| - |A|. Double complement: (A^c)^c = A. De Morgan's: (A union B)^c = A^c inter B^c.

dependencies.description: Complement is defined relative to a universal set

compositionRules.yields: De Morgan's laws for sets: (A union B)^c = A^c inter B^c and (A inter B)^c = A^c union B^c

applicabilityPatterns: Find all elements not in a given set
Express set differences and symmetric differences
Apply De Morgan's laws to set expressions

primitives.name: Cartesian Product
primitives.formalStatement: The Cartesian product of A and B is A x B = {(a,b) : a in A, b in B}. For n sets: A_1 x ... x A_n = {(a_1,...,a_n) : a_i in A_i}. |A x B| = |A| * |B|. R^n = R x R x ... x R (n times).
primitives.computationalForm: Generate all ordered pairs: for each a in A, for each b in B, output (a,b). |A x B| = |A| * |B|. Iterated: A x B x C has elements (a,b,c).

dependencies.description: Cartesian product creates a set from two sets

compositionRules.yields: Relations as subsets of Cartesian products: R subset A x B

applicabilityPatterns: Form all possible pairs from two sets
Construct the domain for relations and functions
Build multi-dimensional spaces from one-dimensional sets

primitives.name: Power Set
primitives.formalStatement: The power set P(A) is the set of all subsets of A: P(A) = {S : S subset A}. |P(A)| = 2^|A|. The power set is always strictly larger than the set itself (Cantor's theorem): |P(A)| > |A|.
primitives.computationalForm: Enumerate all subsets: for set of size n, there are 2^n subsets (each element is either in or out). Include emptyset and A itself. Lattice ordering by subset inclusion.

dependencies.description: Power set is defined in terms of subsets

compositionRules.yields: Cantor's theorem: |P(A)| > |A| for any set A, proving no largest cardinal

applicabilityPatterns: Enumerate all subsets of a given set
Count the number of subsets using 2^n
Construct larger sets from existing ones

primitives.name: Relation
primitives.formalStatement: A relation R from A to B is a subset of A x B. We write aRb or (a,b) in R. Properties: reflexive (aRa), symmetric (aRb => bRa), antisymmetric (aRb and bRa => a=b), transitive (aRb and bRc => aRc).
primitives.computationalForm: Represent as set of ordered pairs, or as a boolean matrix M[i][j] = 1 iff (a_i, b_j) in R. Check properties by examining the matrix: reflexive = diagonal all 1, symmetric = M = M^T.

dependencies.description: A relation is a subset of a Cartesian product

compositionRules.yields: Relations as structured subsets of Cartesian products, enabling order theory
compositionRules.example: Divisibility on Z+: 2|6 means (2,6) in R, where R = {(a,b) : a divides b}

applicabilityPatterns: Define a relationship between elements of two sets
Check if a relation has special properties (reflexive, symmetric, transitive)
Formalize order, equivalence, or other structural relationships

primitives.name: Equivalence Relation
primitives.formalStatement: An equivalence relation ~ on set A is a relation that is reflexive (a ~ a), symmetric (a ~ b => b ~ a), and transitive (a ~ b and b ~ c => a ~ c). It partitions A into disjoint equivalence classes [a] = {x in A : x ~ a}.
primitives.computationalForm: Verify three properties. Equivalence classes: [a] = {x : x ~ a}. Partition: A = union of disjoint equivalence classes. Number of classes = number of distinct representatives.

dependencies.description: Equivalence relation is a special kind of relation

compositionRules.yields: Quotient group: G/N uses equivalence classes (cosets) as group elements
conditions: Normal subgroup N of group G
compositionRules.example: Z/2Z: equivalence classes {even integers} and {odd integers}

applicabilityPatterns: Classify elements into groups where they are considered equivalent
Partition a set into disjoint equivalence classes
Define modular arithmetic or congruence relations
buildLabs: Build Lab 18.2: Equivalence Relations and Partitions

primitives.name: Equivalence Class / Partition Theorem
primitives.formalStatement: An equivalence relation ~ on A determines a unique partition of A into equivalence classes. Conversely, any partition of A determines a unique equivalence relation. The equivalence classes are disjoint and their union is A.
primitives.computationalForm: Given ~: compute [a] for each a. Remove duplicates. Verify: classes are disjoint, union = A. Conversely: given partition {B_i}, define a ~ b iff a, b in same B_i.

dependencies.description: Theorem connects equivalence relations to partitions

compositionRules.yields: Bijection between equivalence relations on A and partitions of A
compositionRules.example: Parity on Z: ~ defined by a ~ b iff a-b is even, partition into {even, odd}

applicabilityPatterns: Convert between an equivalence relation and its corresponding partition
Prove that equivalence classes are disjoint and exhaustive
Count the number of equivalence classes

primitives.name: Function as Relation
primitives.formalStatement: A function f: A -> B is a relation R subset A x B such that for every a in A, there is exactly one b in B with (a,b) in R. A is the domain, B is the codomain, f(A) is the image. Functions can be injective (one-to-one), surjective (onto), or bijective (both).
primitives.computationalForm: Check: for each a in A, count elements b with (a,b) in R; must be exactly 1. Injective: f(a_1) = f(a_2) => a_1 = a_2. Surjective: for all b in B, exists a with f(a) = b. Bijective: both.

dependencies.description: A function is a special kind of relation

compositionRules.yields: Bijection establishes equal cardinality: |A| = |B| iff exists bijection f: A -> B
compositionRules.example: f(n) = 2n is bijection from N to even numbers, so |N| = |{even N}|

applicabilityPatterns: Define a mapping between sets formally
Determine if a function is injective, surjective, or bijective
Establish correspondence between sets

primitives.name: Cardinality
primitives.formalStatement: The cardinality |A| of a set A measures its size. Two sets have the same cardinality iff there exists a bijection between them. Finite sets have cardinality in N. The natural numbers have cardinality aleph_0 (countably infinite). The reals have cardinality c = 2^{aleph_0} > aleph_0.
primitives.computationalForm: Finite: count elements. Countable: construct bijection with N (list all elements). Uncountable: prove no bijection with N exists (diagonalization). Cantor's theorem: |P(A)| > |A| for any A.

dependencies.description: Cardinality comparison uses bijections
Cardinality is a property of sets

compositionRules.yields: Cantor's theorem: no set can be mapped bijectively onto its power set
compositionRules.example: |R| = |P(N)| = 2^{aleph_0} > aleph_0 = |N| (continuum is uncountable)

applicabilityPatterns: Compare the sizes of infinite sets
Prove a set is countable or uncountable
Establish cardinality hierarchies

primitives.name: Cantor's Theorem
primitives.formalStatement: For any set A, there is no surjection from A onto P(A). Consequently |P(A)| > |A| for every set A. In particular, the real numbers are uncountable: |R| = |P(N)| > |N| = aleph_0.
primitives.computationalForm: Proof by diagonalization: suppose f: A -> P(A) is surjective. Define D = {a in A : a not in f(a)}. D is a subset of A, so D in P(A). Then D = f(d) for some d. If d in D then d not in f(d) = D: contradiction. If d not in D then d in f(d) = D: contradiction.

dependencies.description: Cantor's theorem is about comparing cardinalities
Theorem compares |A| with |P(A)|

compositionRules.yields: Diagonal argument: powerful technique for proving impossibility results about infinite sets
conditions: Assume a bijection exists, derive contradiction
compositionRules.example: Cantor diagonal: list all reals in (0,1), change n-th digit of n-th number -> new real not in list

applicabilityPatterns: Prove that a set is uncountable
Show that no enumeration of an infinite set is exhaustive
Establish strict cardinality hierarchies

primitives.name: ZFC Axiom System
primitives.formalStatement: Zermelo-Fraenkel set theory with Choice (ZFC) comprises axioms: Extensionality, Empty Set, Pairing, Union, Power Set, Infinity, Separation (Comprehension), Replacement, Foundation (Regularity), and Choice. ZFC is the standard foundation for most of mathematics.
primitives.computationalForm: ZFC provides existence guarantees: empty set exists, unions exist, power sets exist, infinite sets exist (axiom of infinity). Choice: for any collection of nonempty sets, a function selects one element from each. Foundation: no infinite descending membership chains.

compositionRules.yields: ZFC as a first-order theory: axioms + deduction rules determine all provable theorems about sets
compositionRules.example: From ZFC axioms: existence of natural numbers, real numbers, and all standard mathematical objects

applicabilityPatterns: Justify the existence of mathematical objects from axioms
Resolve paradoxes like Russell's paradox via restricted comprehension
Provide the formal foundation for all mathematical constructions

primitives.name: Propositional Logic (Boolean Operations)
primitives.formalStatement: Propositional logic deals with propositions (true/false statements) combined by logical connectives: AND (conjunction, p ^ q), OR (disjunction, p v q), NOT (negation, ~p), IMPLIES (conditional, p -> q), IFF (biconditional, p <-> q).
primitives.computationalForm: Evaluate truth values: p ^ q is true iff both are true. p v q is true iff at least one is true. ~p flips truth value. p -> q is false only when p is true and q is false. Build compound propositions from atomic ones.

dependencies.description: Set operations mirror logical operations

compositionRules.yields: Boolean algebra: logic operations correspond to set operations (AND=intersection, OR=union, NOT=complement)
compositionRules.example: x in (A inter B) iff (x in A) AND (x in B)

applicabilityPatterns: Combine simple statements into complex logical expressions
Determine the truth value of a compound proposition
Formalize arguments and reasoning
buildLabs: Build Lab 19.1: Logic Gates and Truth Tables

primitives.name: Truth Tables
primitives.formalStatement: A truth table systematically lists all possible truth value combinations for the atomic propositions in an expression and the resulting truth value of the expression. For n atomic propositions, the table has 2^n rows.
primitives.computationalForm: 1. List all 2^n combinations of truth values for n variables. 2. Evaluate the expression for each combination. 3. The final column shows the expression's truth value. Use to verify tautologies, contradictions, and equivalences.

dependencies.description: Truth tables evaluate propositional logic expressions

compositionRules.yields: Proof of equivalence: two expressions are equivalent iff their truth tables match on all rows
compositionRules.example: p -> q and ~p v q have identical truth tables (4 rows, all match)

applicabilityPatterns: Determine if a logical expression is a tautology, contradiction, or contingency
Verify logical equivalences systematically
Check the validity of an argument form

primitives.name: Predicate Logic (Quantifiers)
primitives.formalStatement: Predicate logic extends propositional logic with variables, predicates P(x), and quantifiers: universal (forall x, P(x)) meaning P holds for all x, and existential (exists x, P(x)) meaning P holds for some x. Negation: ~(forall x, P(x)) iff (exists x, ~P(x)).
primitives.computationalForm: Evaluate: forall x in D: check P(x) for every x. exists x in D: find at least one x where P(x) is true. Nested quantifiers: evaluate from outside in. Order matters: forall x exists y != exists y forall x in general.

dependencies.description: Predicate logic extends propositional logic with quantifiers

compositionRules.yields: Set comprehension: {x in A : P(x)} uses predicate logic to define sets
compositionRules.example: Prime numbers: {n in N : n > 1 and forall d, (1 < d < n -> d does not divide n)}

applicabilityPatterns: Express mathematical statements involving 'for all' or 'there exists'
Negate quantified statements correctly
Formalize mathematical definitions and theorems

primitives.name: Proof by Direct Deduction
primitives.formalStatement: A direct proof of 'P implies Q' assumes P is true and derives Q through a chain of valid logical steps. Each step follows from previous steps, axioms, or previously proven theorems.
primitives.computationalForm: Structure: 1. Assume P. 2. Apply definitions, axioms, and previously proven results. 3. Derive intermediate conclusions. 4. Conclude Q. Each step must be logically justified.

dependencies.description: Direct proof uses logical deduction rules

compositionRules.yields: Chain of implications: P -> Q, Q -> R gives P -> R via transitivity
compositionRules.example: Prove 'if n is even, then n^2 is even': Assume n=2k. Then n^2=4k^2=2(2k^2), which is even.

applicabilityPatterns: Prove a conditional statement by assuming the hypothesis and deriving the conclusion
Build a logical argument from known facts to desired conclusion
Establish a mathematical result through straightforward reasoning

primitives.name: Proof by Contradiction
primitives.formalStatement: To prove P, assume ~P and derive a contradiction (a statement that is both true and false). Since ~P leads to a contradiction, ~P must be false, so P is true. This relies on the law of excluded middle: P or ~P.
primitives.computationalForm: Structure: 1. Assume ~P (negation of what you want to prove). 2. Apply logical deductions. 3. Arrive at a statement R and ~R (contradiction). 4. Conclude P.

dependencies.description: Uses logical deduction and negation

compositionRules.yields: Proving nonexistence or impossibility: assume it exists, derive contradiction
compositionRules.example: sqrt(2) is irrational: assume sqrt(2) = p/q (lowest terms), then 2q^2 = p^2, so p is even, then q is even, contradicting lowest terms

applicabilityPatterns: Prove a statement by showing its negation is impossible
Establish irrationality, nonexistence, or impossibility results
Handle cases where direct proof is difficult

primitives.name: Proof by Induction
primitives.formalStatement: Mathematical induction proves P(n) for all n >= n_0: (1) Base case: prove P(n_0). (2) Inductive step: prove that P(k) implies P(k+1) for arbitrary k >= n_0. Strong induction: assume P(j) for all n_0 <= j <= k, prove P(k+1).
primitives.computationalForm: 1. Verify P(n_0) directly. 2. Assume P(k) for arbitrary k (inductive hypothesis). 3. Use the hypothesis to prove P(k+1). 4. Conclude: by induction, P(n) holds for all n >= n_0.

dependencies.description: Induction is a proof technique using quantified statements

compositionRules.yields: Universal statements about natural numbers: proves P(n) for all n simultaneously
conditions: Property depends on natural number n
compositionRules.example: Sum formula: 1+2+...+n = n(n+1)/2. Base: n=1, 1=1. Step: assume for k, then 1+...+k+(k+1) = k(k+1)/2 + (k+1) = (k+1)(k+2)/2.

applicabilityPatterns: Prove a statement holds for all natural numbers
Establish formulas, inequalities, or properties by building from base case
Prove properties of recursively defined structures

primitives.name: Proof by Contrapositive
primitives.formalStatement: To prove 'P implies Q', instead prove '~Q implies ~P' (the contrapositive). The contrapositive is logically equivalent to the original implication: (P -> Q) iff (~Q -> ~P).
primitives.computationalForm: Structure: 1. Assume ~Q (negation of conclusion). 2. Derive ~P (negation of hypothesis). 3. Conclude P -> Q by contrapositive equivalence.

dependencies.description: Contrapositive relies on logical equivalence of P->Q and ~Q->~P

compositionRules.yields: Alternative proof strategy: sometimes ~Q -> ~P is easier than P -> Q directly
conditions: Original implication is hard to prove directly
compositionRules.example: Prove: if n^2 is odd then n is odd. Contrapositive: if n is even then n^2 is even. Proof: n=2k => n^2=4k^2=2(2k^2), even.

applicabilityPatterns: Prove an implication by proving its equivalent contrapositive
Handle cases where the conclusion is hard to derive directly
Use negations to simplify the proof structure

primitives.name: Logical Equivalence
primitives.formalStatement: Two propositions P and Q are logically equivalent (P <=> Q) if they have the same truth value in every possible interpretation. Equivalently, P <=> Q iff (P -> Q) and (Q -> P). Key equivalences: P -> Q <=> ~P v Q, ~(P ^ Q) <=> ~P v ~Q.
primitives.computationalForm: Check equivalence via truth tables: P <=> Q iff truth tables have identical final columns. Or prove both directions: P -> Q and Q -> P.

dependencies.description: Logical equivalence is a relation between propositions

compositionRules.yields: Mechanical verification of equivalence: compare truth table columns
compositionRules.example: P -> Q <=> ~P v Q: both false only when P=T, Q=F

applicabilityPatterns: Simplify logical expressions by replacing with equivalent forms
Verify that two different formulations say the same thing
Transform implications into disjunctions for easier reasoning

primitives.name: Tautology and Contradiction
primitives.formalStatement: A tautology is a proposition that is true under every possible assignment of truth values to its variables. A contradiction is a proposition that is false under every assignment. A contingency is neither. Example tautology: P v ~P. Example contradiction: P ^ ~P.
primitives.computationalForm: Check via truth table: if all rows give TRUE, it is a tautology. If all rows give FALSE, it is a contradiction. Tautologies are logically valid formulas.

dependencies.description: Tautologies and contradictions are propositional logic concepts

compositionRules.yields: Contradiction detection: if an assumption leads to P ^ ~P, the assumption is false
compositionRules.example: Assume sqrt(2) = p/q -> leads to P='p is even' and 'q is even' contradicting 'gcd(p,q)=1'

applicabilityPatterns: Identify logical formulas that are always true or always false
Verify the validity of argument forms
Recognize contradictions to apply proof by contradiction

primitives.name: De Morgan's Laws
primitives.formalStatement: For propositions: ~(P ^ Q) <=> (~P v ~Q) and ~(P v Q) <=> (~P ^ ~Q). For sets: (A inter B)^c = A^c union B^c and (A union B)^c = A^c inter B^c. These laws connect negation with conjunction and disjunction.
primitives.computationalForm: To negate a conjunction: negate each part and change AND to OR. To negate a disjunction: negate each part and change OR to AND. Extends to n terms: ~(P_1 ^ ... ^ P_n) = ~P_1 v ... v ~P_n.

dependencies.description: De Morgan's laws are logical equivalences
Set version uses complement operation

compositionRules.yields: Quantifier negation: ~(forall x, P(x)) <=> exists x, ~P(x) (generalized De Morgan's)
compositionRules.example: 'Not all students passed' <=> 'some student did not pass'

applicabilityPatterns: Negate complex logical expressions systematically
Convert between union/intersection under complement
Simplify logical formulas by pushing negations inward

primitives.name: Modus Ponens
primitives.formalStatement: Modus ponens is the fundamental rule of inference: from P and P -> Q, conclude Q. In symbols: {P, P -> Q} |- Q. It is the basis of all forward reasoning in logic.
primitives.computationalForm: Given: premise P is true, and conditional P -> Q is true. Conclude: Q is true. Chain: if also Q -> R, then conclude R (hypothetical syllogism).

compositionRules.yields: Direct proof chains: P -> Q, Q -> R gives P -> R via repeated modus ponens
compositionRules.example: If it rains, the ground is wet. It rains. Therefore the ground is wet.

applicabilityPatterns: Apply a conditional rule to derive a new fact
Chain logical implications together
Implement the basic step of logical deduction

primitives.name: Formal Axiomatic System
primitives.formalStatement: A formal axiomatic system consists of: (1) a language (symbols, terms, formulas), (2) axioms (accepted truths), (3) rules of inference (ways to derive new theorems from axioms and existing theorems). A system is consistent if it cannot prove both P and ~P. It is complete if every true statement is provable.
primitives.computationalForm: Define: alphabet, formation rules (well-formed formulas), axioms (initial theorems), inference rules. A proof is a finite sequence of formulas, each an axiom or derived from previous ones by inference rules. A theorem is the last formula in a proof.

dependencies.description: Formal systems are built on formal logic

compositionRules.yields: Fundamental limitation: no consistent system rich enough for arithmetic can prove all truths about arithmetic
compositionRules.example: Peano arithmetic: consistent and sound, but incomplete (by Godel's theorems)

applicabilityPatterns: Formalize a mathematical theory with explicit axioms and rules
Determine what can and cannot be proved in a given system
Study the foundations of mathematical reasoning

primitives.name: Godel's Incompleteness Theorems
primitives.formalStatement: First theorem: Any consistent formal system F capable of expressing basic arithmetic contains statements that are true but unprovable in F. Second theorem: F cannot prove its own consistency (if it is in fact consistent).
primitives.computationalForm: Construct a Godel sentence G that says 'I am not provable in F'. If F is consistent: G is true but unprovable (if G were provable, F proves something false, contradicting consistency). This shows F is incomplete. Adding G as an axiom creates a new system with its own Godel sentence.

dependencies.description: Incompleteness is a property of formal axiomatic systems

compositionRules.yields: Fundamental limits of formalization: no finite axiom system can capture all mathematical truth
compositionRules.example: Peano arithmetic cannot prove Con(PA), the statement of its own consistency

applicabilityPatterns: Understand the inherent limitations of formal mathematical systems
Explain why no single theory can prove all mathematical truths
Recognize self-referential constructions in logic

primitives.name: Group Definition and Axioms
primitives.formalStatement: A group (G, *) is a set G with a binary operation * satisfying: (1) Closure: a*b in G for all a,b in G. (2) Associativity: (a*b)*c = a*(b*c). (3) Identity: exists e in G such that e*a = a*e = a. (4) Inverses: for each a, exists a^{-1} with a*a^{-1} = a^{-1}*a = e.
primitives.computationalForm: Verify group axioms: check closure (operation table stays in G), associativity (check all triples), identity element exists, every element has an inverse. Abelian group: additionally a*b = b*a for all a,b.

compositionRules.yields: Group homomorphisms preserve group structure: f(a*b) = f(a)*f(b)
conditions: Both domain and codomain are groups
compositionRules.example: exp: (R, +) -> (R+, *) is a group homomorphism: exp(a+b) = exp(a)*exp(b)

applicabilityPatterns: Verify if a set with an operation forms a group
Identify symmetries of objects as group elements
Study algebraic structures with a single binary operation

primitives.name: Subgroup
primitives.formalStatement: A subset H of group G is a subgroup if H is itself a group under the same operation. Equivalently (subgroup test): H is nonempty, and for all a, b in H: a*b^{-1} in H.
primitives.computationalForm: Subgroup test: 1. Check H is nonempty. 2. For all a, b in H, check a*b^{-1} in H. If both pass, H is a subgroup. Index [G:H] = |G|/|H| (for finite groups, by Lagrange's theorem).

dependencies.description: Subgroup inherits group structure

compositionRules.yields: Subgroup order divides group order: |H| divides |G|
conditions: H is a subgroup of G
compositionRules.example: S_3 has order 6; subgroups have order 1, 2, 3, or 6 (all divisors of 6)

applicabilityPatterns: Identify a subset of a group that is itself a group
Find all subgroups of a given group
Determine the index of a subgroup

primitives.name: Homomorphism
primitives.formalStatement: A group homomorphism f: G -> H is a function satisfying f(a *_G b) = f(a) *_H f(b) for all a, b in G. It preserves the group operation. The kernel ker(f) = {a in G : f(a) = e_H} is a normal subgroup of G. The image im(f) is a subgroup of H.
primitives.computationalForm: Verify: f(a*b) = f(a)*f(b) for all a, b. Kernel: find all elements mapping to identity. Image: compute f(a) for all a in G. Properties: f(e_G) = e_H, f(a^{-1}) = f(a)^{-1}.

dependencies.description: Homomorphism is defined between groups
A homomorphism is a function with additional structure

compositionRules.yields: Structure theorem: G/ker(f) is isomorphic to im(f) (First Isomorphism Theorem)
compositionRules.example: f: Z -> Z/nZ by f(k) = k mod n; ker(f) = nZ, im(f) = Z/nZ, and Z/nZ ≅ Z/nZ

applicabilityPatterns: Define a structure-preserving map between groups
Identify the kernel and image of a group map
Classify groups up to homomorphic relationships

primitives.name: Isomorphism
primitives.formalStatement: A group isomorphism f: G -> H is a bijective homomorphism. Two groups are isomorphic (G ≅ H) if an isomorphism exists between them. Isomorphic groups have identical algebraic structure: same order, same number of elements of each order, same subgroup lattice.
primitives.computationalForm: Check: f is bijective and f(a*b) = f(a)*f(b). Isomorphic groups: match elements by order, check operation tables match. Classification: up to isomorphism, identify distinct group structures.

dependencies.description: Isomorphism is a special homomorphism

compositionRules.yields: Group classification: identify all groups of a given order up to isomorphism
compositionRules.example: Groups of order 4: Z/4Z and Z/2Z x Z/2Z (only two, up to isomorphism)

applicabilityPatterns: Determine if two groups have the same algebraic structure
Classify groups by identifying isomorphism classes
Transfer results between isomorphic groups

primitives.name: Ring Definition
primitives.formalStatement: A ring (R, +, *) is a set with two binary operations: (R, +) is an abelian group, (R, *) is a monoid (associative with identity 1), and * distributes over +: a*(b+c) = a*b + a*c and (a+b)*c = a*c + b*c.
primitives.computationalForm: Verify: (R, +) is abelian group, * is associative with identity, left and right distributivity hold. Commutative ring: a*b = b*a. Examples: Z, Z/nZ, polynomial ring R[x], matrix ring M_n(R).

dependencies.description: (R, +) must be an abelian group

compositionRules.yields: Fields are commutative rings where every nonzero element has a multiplicative inverse
conditions: Ring is commutative and every nonzero element is a unit
compositionRules.example: Z is a ring but not a field (2 has no multiplicative inverse). Q is a field.

applicabilityPatterns: Study algebraic structures with two operations (addition and multiplication)
Determine if a set with two operations forms a ring
Work with polynomial rings, matrix rings, and modular arithmetic

primitives.name: Field Definition
primitives.formalStatement: A field (F, +, *) is a commutative ring where every nonzero element has a multiplicative inverse: for all a != 0, exists a^{-1} with a*a^{-1} = 1. Equivalently, (F\{0}, *) is an abelian group. Examples: Q, R, C, Z/pZ (p prime), finite fields F_q.
primitives.computationalForm: Verify: (F, +) abelian group, (F\{0}, *) abelian group, distributivity. In a field: division is always possible (except by 0). Characteristic: smallest positive n with n*1 = 0 (0 if no such n).

dependencies.description: A field is a special kind of ring

compositionRules.yields: A field has two group structures: (F, +) and (F\{0}, *), connected by distributivity
compositionRules.example: R: (R, +) is additive group; (R\{0}, *) is multiplicative group; multiplication distributes over addition

applicabilityPatterns: Determine if an algebraic structure is a field
Work with number systems where division is possible
Build vector spaces over a field

primitives.name: Quotient Group (Normal Subgroup)
primitives.formalStatement: If N is a normal subgroup of G (gNg^{-1} = N for all g), the quotient group G/N consists of cosets gN = {gn : n in N} with operation (gN)(hN) = (gh)N. |G/N| = |G|/|N| = [G:N].
primitives.computationalForm: 1. Verify N is normal: gNg^{-1} = N for all g. 2. Form cosets: gN for each g in G. 3. Identify distinct cosets (gN = hN iff g^{-1}h in N). 4. Define multiplication: (gN)(hN) = (gh)N.

dependencies.description: Normal subgroup is a special subgroup
Cosets form equivalence classes

compositionRules.yields: G/ker(f) ≅ im(f): quotient by kernel gives the image

applicabilityPatterns: Construct a new group by collapsing a normal subgroup to the identity
Simplify group structure by modding out a subgroup
Build modular arithmetic as a quotient group

primitives.name: Lagrange's Theorem
primitives.formalStatement: For a finite group G and subgroup H: |H| divides |G|. The index [G:H] = |G|/|H| equals the number of distinct cosets of H in G. Consequence: the order of every element divides the order of the group.
primitives.computationalForm: |G| = |H| * [G:H]. For element a of order d: d divides |G|. Possible subgroup orders divide |G|. This constrains which subgroups can exist.

dependencies.description: Theorem relates subgroup order to group order

compositionRules.yields: Order constraints: possible element orders and subgroup orders of a finite group
compositionRules.example: Group of order 12: element orders can be 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, or 12; subgroup orders can be 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, or 12

applicabilityPatterns: Determine which subgroup orders are possible for a finite group
Prove that element order divides group order
Count the number of cosets of a subgroup

primitives.name: First Isomorphism Theorem
primitives.formalStatement: For a group homomorphism f: G -> H, the quotient G/ker(f) is isomorphic to im(f): G/ker(f) ≅ im(f). The map gN -> f(g) is a well-defined isomorphism.
primitives.computationalForm: Given f: G -> H. 1. Compute ker(f) = {g : f(g) = e_H}. 2. Form quotient G/ker(f). 3. The map phi: G/ker(f) -> im(f) by phi(gN) = f(g) is an isomorphism. |im(f)| = |G|/|ker(f)|.

dependencies.description: Theorem is about group homomorphisms
Uses quotient group construction
Conclusion is an isomorphism

compositionRules.yields: Factor any homomorphism through a quotient: f = injection o canonical projection
compositionRules.example: f: Z -> Z/5Z by f(n) = n mod 5: ker = 5Z, Z/5Z ≅ Z/5Z

applicabilityPatterns: Relate the quotient by a kernel to the image of a homomorphism
Determine the structure of a quotient group
Factor homomorphisms through quotient constructions

primitives.name: Cyclic Group and Generators
primitives.formalStatement: A group G is cyclic if G = <a> = {a^n : n in Z} for some generator a. Every cyclic group is isomorphic to Z (infinite) or Z/nZ (finite of order n). Cyclic groups are always abelian. Every subgroup of a cyclic group is cyclic.
primitives.computationalForm: Find generator: element a such that powers of a produce all group elements. For Z/nZ: generators are elements coprime to n. Number of generators = phi(n) (Euler's totient). Subgroups of Z/nZ: one for each divisor of n.

dependencies.description: Cyclic groups are a special class of groups

compositionRules.yields: In a cyclic group of order n, there is exactly one subgroup of order d for each divisor d of n
compositionRules.example: Z/12Z: subgroups of order 1,2,3,4,6,12 generated by 0,6,4,3,2,1 respectively

applicabilityPatterns: Determine if a group is generated by a single element
Classify all cyclic groups and their subgroups
Work with modular arithmetic as cyclic group operations

primitives.name: Symmetry Group (Permutation Group)
primitives.formalStatement: The symmetric group S_n is the group of all permutations of {1,...,n} under composition. |S_n| = n!. Every group is isomorphic to a subgroup of some S_n (Cayley's theorem). Dihedral group D_n: symmetries of regular n-gon, |D_n| = 2n.
primitives.computationalForm: Represent permutations as functions or cycle notation. Compose by applying right-to-left. Order of a permutation = LCM of cycle lengths. S_n generators: any transposition and any n-cycle.

dependencies.description: Symmetric group is a group under composition

compositionRules.yields: Group actions on sets: S_n naturally acts on {1,...,n} by permuting elements
compositionRules.example: D_3 acts on triangle vertices: rotations and reflections permute {1,2,3}

applicabilityPatterns: Study symmetries of geometric objects as group elements
Represent any group as a permutation group (Cayley's theorem)
Count arrangements and symmetric configurations
buildLabs: Build Lab 20.2: Symmetry Groups of Regular Polygons

primitives.name: Galois Theory Connection
primitives.formalStatement: Galois theory establishes a correspondence between field extensions and groups: the Galois group Gal(E/F) consists of field automorphisms of E fixing F. The Fundamental Theorem of Galois Theory: subgroups of Gal(E/F) correspond bijectively to intermediate fields between F and E.
primitives.computationalForm: For polynomial p(x) over F: splitting field E = F(roots of p). Gal(E/F) = automorphisms of E fixing F. Subgroup H corresponds to fixed field E^H. Application: p(x) is solvable by radicals iff Gal(E/F) is a solvable group.

dependencies.description: Galois theory connects fields and groups
Galois groups are often subgroups of symmetric groups

compositionRules.yields: Unsolvability of the quintic: S_5 is not solvable, so general degree-5 polynomials have no radical formula
compositionRules.example: x^5 - x - 1: Galois group is S_5, which is not solvable -> no formula in radicals

applicabilityPatterns: Determine if a polynomial equation is solvable by radicals
Study the symmetries of polynomial roots
Connect field extensions to group theory

primitives.name: Group Action
primitives.formalStatement: A group action of G on a set X is a function G x X -> X written g.x satisfying: (1) e.x = x for all x (identity acts trivially), (2) (gh).x = g.(h.x) for all g,h,x (compatibility). The orbit of x is O_x = {g.x : g in G}. The stabilizer of x is G_x = {g : g.x = x}.
primitives.computationalForm: Orbit-stabilizer theorem: |O_x| * |G_x| = |G|. Burnside's lemma: number of orbits = (1/|G|) sum_{g in G} |Fix(g)|. Faithful action: only identity fixes all points.

dependencies.description: Group actions require a group acting on a set

compositionRules.yields: Counting symmetric configurations via Burnside's lemma
conditions: Finite group acting on finite set
compositionRules.example: Color cube faces with 2 colors: rotation group has 24 elements, Burnside gives 10 distinct colorings

applicabilityPatterns: Model how a group of symmetries transforms a set
Count distinct objects up to symmetry equivalence
Decompose a set into orbits under a group action

primitives.name: Kernel and Image
primitives.formalStatement: For a group homomorphism f: G -> H: the kernel ker(f) = {g in G : f(g) = e_H} is a normal subgroup of G. The image im(f) = {f(g) : g in G} is a subgroup of H. f is injective iff ker(f) = {e_G}. f is surjective iff im(f) = H.
primitives.computationalForm: Compute kernel: find all elements mapping to identity. Compute image: apply f to all elements. |im(f)| = [G : ker(f)] (by first isomorphism theorem). Test injectivity: check ker(f) = {e}.

dependencies.description: Kernel and image are defined for homomorphisms

compositionRules.yields: G/ker(f) ≅ im(f) (first isomorphism theorem)
compositionRules.example: det: GL(n,R) -> R*: kernel = SL(n,R) (matrices with det=1), image = R*

applicabilityPatterns: Find elements that map to the identity under a homomorphism
Determine the range of a group homomorphism
Test if a homomorphism is injective or surjective

primitives.name: Topological Space
primitives.formalStatement: A topological space (X, tau) is a set X with a collection tau of subsets (called open sets) satisfying: (1) emptyset and X are in tau. (2) Any union of sets in tau is in tau. (3) Any finite intersection of sets in tau is in tau.
primitives.computationalForm: Verify topology axioms: check empty set and X are open, arbitrary unions of open sets are open, finite intersections of open sets are open. Common topologies: discrete (all subsets open), trivial ({emptyset, X}), metric topology, product topology.

compositionRules.yields: Continuous functions: f: X -> Y is continuous iff preimage of every open set is open
conditions: Both X and Y are topological spaces
compositionRules.example: f: R -> R continuous iff f^{-1}(a,b) is open for every interval (a,b)

applicabilityPatterns: Define the concept of 'nearness' or 'openness' without a metric
Study properties preserved under continuous deformation
Generalize analysis to abstract settings

primitives.name: Open Set and Closed Set
primitives.formalStatement: In a topological space (X, tau), a set U is open if U in tau. A set C is closed if X \ C is open. The closure cl(A) is the smallest closed set containing A. The interior int(A) is the largest open set contained in A. A set can be both open and closed (clopen).
primitives.computationalForm: Open: check membership in tau. Closed: check complement is in tau. Closure: cl(A) = intersection of all closed sets containing A. Interior: int(A) = union of all open sets contained in A. Boundary: bd(A) = cl(A) \ int(A).

dependencies.description: Open and closed are defined relative to a topology

compositionRules.yields: Metric topology: open balls B(x,r) = {y : d(x,y) < r} generate the metric topology
compositionRules.example: In R: open intervals (a,b) are open sets, closed intervals [a,b] are closed sets

applicabilityPatterns: Determine if a set is open, closed, or neither in a given topology
Compute the closure, interior, and boundary of a set
Work with topological properties defined via open/closed sets

primitives.name: Continuity in Topology
primitives.formalStatement: A function f: X -> Y between topological spaces is continuous if for every open set V in Y, the preimage f^{-1}(V) is open in X. This generalizes the epsilon-delta definition: in metric spaces, the two definitions are equivalent.
primitives.computationalForm: Check: for each open V in Y, verify f^{-1}(V) is open in X. Equivalently: for each closed C in Y, f^{-1}(C) is closed in X. In metric spaces: for every epsilon > 0, exists delta > 0 such that d_X(x, x_0) < delta implies d_Y(f(x), f(x_0)) < epsilon.

dependencies.description: Continuity is defined between topological spaces
Defined via preimages of open sets

compositionRules.yields: Topological equivalence: continuous bijection with continuous inverse
conditions: f is bijective and both f and f^{-1} are continuous
compositionRules.example: f(t) = (cos t, sin t): [0, 2pi) -> circle is continuous bijection, but not homeomorphism (inverse not continuous)

applicabilityPatterns: Determine if a function between topological spaces is continuous
Generalize continuity from metric spaces to arbitrary topologies
Preserve topological properties under continuous maps

primitives.name: Homeomorphism
primitives.formalStatement: A homeomorphism f: X -> Y is a continuous bijection whose inverse f^{-1}: Y -> X is also continuous. Two spaces are homeomorphic (X ≅ Y) if a homeomorphism exists between them. Homeomorphic spaces are topologically equivalent — they share all topological properties.
primitives.computationalForm: Verify: f is bijective, f is continuous, f^{-1} is continuous. Topological invariants preserved by homeomorphism: compactness, connectedness, fundamental group, Euler characteristic. Not invariant: metric properties (distance, angle).

dependencies.description: Homeomorphism requires continuity in both directions

compositionRules.yields: Distinguish non-homeomorphic surfaces by computing their Euler characteristics
compositionRules.example: Sphere (chi=2) is not homeomorphic to torus (chi=0) because they have different Euler characteristics

applicabilityPatterns: Determine if two topological spaces are equivalent
Classify spaces up to topological equivalence
Prove two spaces are NOT homeomorphic by finding different invariants
buildLabs: Build Lab 21.2: Homeomorphisms and Rubber-Sheet Geometry

primitives.name: Compactness
primitives.formalStatement: A topological space X is compact if every open cover has a finite subcover: if X = union_{alpha} U_alpha with each U_alpha open, then X = U_{alpha_1} union ... union U_{alpha_n} for finitely many alpha_i. In R^n, compact = closed and bounded (Heine-Borel).
primitives.computationalForm: Check compactness: for every open cover, extract a finite subcover. In metric spaces: equivalent to sequentially compact (every sequence has convergent subsequence). For R^n subsets: check closed and bounded.

dependencies.description: Compactness is a topological property
Defined via open covers

compositionRules.yields: Continuous image of compact space is compact; continuous functions on compact spaces attain their bounds
compositionRules.example: f: [a,b] -> R continuous: f attains max and min on [a,b] (extreme value theorem)

applicabilityPatterns: Determine if a space has the compactness property
Ensure continuous functions attain their maximum and minimum
Extract finite information from infinite covers

primitives.name: Connectedness
primitives.formalStatement: A topological space X is connected if it cannot be written as a union of two nonempty disjoint open sets. Equivalently: the only clopen subsets are emptyset and X. Path-connected: any two points can be joined by a continuous path.
primitives.computationalForm: Check: does there exist a nontrivial partition of X into two open sets? If yes: disconnected. If no: connected. Path-connected implies connected, but not conversely (in general). In R^n: connected iff path-connected (for open sets).

dependencies.description: Connectedness is a topological property
Defined via open/closed sets

compositionRules.yields: Intermediate value theorem generalization: continuous image of connected space is connected
compositionRules.example: f: [0,1] -> R continuous, f(0)=0, f(1)=1: image contains all values between 0 and 1 (IVT)

applicabilityPatterns: Determine if a topological space consists of one piece
Prove existence of solutions via intermediate value theorem
Classify spaces by their number of connected components

primitives.name: Hausdorff Space
primitives.formalStatement: A topological space is Hausdorff (T2) if for any two distinct points x != y, there exist disjoint open sets U, V with x in U and y in V. In Hausdorff spaces, limits of sequences are unique, and compact subsets are closed.
primitives.computationalForm: Check: for every pair of distinct points, find separating open neighborhoods. All metric spaces are Hausdorff (use balls of radius d(x,y)/2). Consequences: singletons are closed, limits are unique.

dependencies.description: Hausdorff is a separation axiom for topological spaces

compositionRules.yields: In Hausdorff spaces: compact subsets are closed, and compact Hausdorff spaces have nice properties (normality, Urysohn's lemma applies)
compositionRules.example: R is Hausdorff; [0,1] is compact and therefore closed in R

applicabilityPatterns: Ensure that limits are unique in a topological space
Separate points with open neighborhoods
Work with topological spaces that behave like metric spaces

primitives.name: Metric Space
primitives.formalStatement: A metric space (X, d) is a set X with a distance function d: X x X -> [0, infinity) satisfying: (1) d(x,y) = 0 iff x = y, (2) d(x,y) = d(y,x) (symmetry), (3) d(x,z) <= d(x,y) + d(y,z) (triangle inequality).
primitives.computationalForm: Common metrics: Euclidean d_2(x,y) = sqrt(sum (x_i-y_i)^2), Manhattan d_1(x,y) = sum |x_i-y_i|, sup-metric d_inf(x,y) = max |x_i-y_i|. Open ball: B(x,r) = {y : d(x,y) < r}.

dependencies.description: Every metric space has a natural topology (metric topology)

compositionRules.yields: In metric spaces: compact = complete and totally bounded = sequentially compact
compositionRules.example: R^n: compact iff closed and bounded (Heine-Borel theorem)

applicabilityPatterns: Define distance between points in a structured way
Work with convergence and continuity using explicit distances
Build topological spaces from distance functions

primitives.name: Fundamental Group
primitives.formalStatement: The fundamental group pi_1(X, x_0) of a topological space X at basepoint x_0 is the group of homotopy classes of loops based at x_0, with group operation being path concatenation. It measures the 'holes' in X: pi_1(S^1) = Z (one loop type per winding number), pi_1(R^n) = 0 (no holes).
primitives.computationalForm: Two loops are equivalent if one can be continuously deformed into the other. Identity: constant loop. Inverse: reversed loop. Concatenation: traverse first loop then second. Compute: for simple spaces, use generators and relations.

dependencies.description: Fundamental group is a topological invariant
Fundamental group is defined via homotopy classes
Fundamental group is a group

compositionRules.yields: Algebraic topology toolkit: fundamental group + Euler characteristic distinguish most surfaces
compositionRules.example: Torus: pi_1 = Z x Z, chi = 0. Klein bottle: pi_1 = Z semidirect Z, chi = 0.

applicabilityPatterns: Detect holes in a topological space
Distinguish non-homeomorphic spaces via their fundamental groups
Classify surfaces and knots using algebraic invariants

primitives.name: Euler Characteristic
primitives.formalStatement: The Euler characteristic of a polyhedron or cell complex is chi = V - E + F (vertices minus edges plus faces). For closed surfaces: sphere chi = 2, torus chi = 0, genus-g surface chi = 2 - 2g. Chi is a topological invariant: homeomorphic spaces have the same chi.
primitives.computationalForm: For a triangulated surface: count V, E, F, compute chi = V - E + F. For genus-g orientable surface: chi = 2 - 2g. Check: same chi is necessary (but not sufficient) for homeomorphism.

dependencies.description: Euler characteristic is a topological invariant
Defined for topological objects

compositionRules.yields: Surface classification: genus g determines both chi and pi_1
compositionRules.example: Genus 1 (torus): chi = 0, pi_1 = Z x Z. Genus 2 (double torus): chi = -2, pi_1 has 4 generators.

applicabilityPatterns: Compute a numerical invariant of a surface or polyhedron
Distinguish surfaces by their Euler characteristic
Verify Euler's formula for polyhedra: V - E + F = 2

primitives.name: Homotopy
primitives.formalStatement: Two continuous maps f, g: X -> Y are homotopic (f ~ g) if there exists a continuous function H: X x [0,1] -> Y with H(x,0) = f(x) and H(x,1) = g(x). Homotopy is an equivalence relation. Two spaces are homotopy equivalent if there exist maps f: X -> Y and g: Y -> X with g o f ~ id_X and f o g ~ id_Y.
primitives.computationalForm: Construct H(x,t) that continuously deforms f into g as t goes from 0 to 1. Linear homotopy: H(x,t) = (1-t)*f(x) + t*g(x) (in R^n). Homotopy equivalence is weaker than homeomorphism.

dependencies.description: Homotopy is defined via continuous deformations

compositionRules.yields: Homotopy type: spaces that are continuously deformable into each other share algebraic invariants
compositionRules.example: R^n is homotopy equivalent to a point (contractible): H(x,t) = (1-t)*x continuously shrinks R^n to 0

applicabilityPatterns: Determine if two continuous maps can be continuously deformed into each other
Classify spaces up to continuous deformation
Define algebraic invariants via homotopy classes

primitives.name: Topological Invariant
primitives.formalStatement: A topological invariant is a property or quantity that is preserved by homeomorphisms. Examples: compactness, connectedness, fundamental group, Euler characteristic, number of holes, orientability. If two spaces differ in any topological invariant, they are not homeomorphic.
primitives.computationalForm: To show X not homeomorphic to Y: find an invariant that differs. Compactness: [0,1] compact, (0,1) not -> not homeomorphic. Fundamental group: S^1 has pi_1 = Z, [0,1] has pi_1 = 0 -> not homeomorphic.

dependencies.description: Invariants are preserved by homeomorphisms
Euler characteristic is a key invariant

compositionRules.yields: Algebraic topology: use algebraic invariants (groups, numbers) to distinguish topological spaces
compositionRules.example: Sphere (pi_1=0, chi=2), torus (pi_1=ZxZ, chi=0), projective plane (pi_1=Z/2Z, chi=1): all distinct

applicabilityPatterns: Distinguish non-homeomorphic spaces using invariant properties
Classify topological spaces using computable invariants
Prove that no homeomorphism can exist between two spaces

primitives.name: Product Topology
primitives.formalStatement: The product topology on X x Y is the topology generated by sets U x V where U is open in X and V is open in Y. Projections pi_X: X x Y -> X and pi_Y: X x Y -> Y are continuous. For infinite products, the product topology uses finite intersections of preimages of open sets.
primitives.computationalForm: Basis: products U x V of open sets from each factor. A set is open in X x Y iff it is a union of such basis elements. Continuous maps into X x Y: f is continuous iff pi_X o f and pi_Y o f are continuous.

dependencies.description: Product topology is defined on products of topological spaces
Underlying set is the Cartesian product

compositionRules.yields: R^n with standard topology = product of n copies of R with standard topology
compositionRules.example: R^2 = R x R with product topology = standard Euclidean topology on the plane

applicabilityPatterns: Define topology on a product of spaces
Construct higher-dimensional spaces from lower-dimensional ones
Verify continuity of functions into product spaces via projections

primitives.name: Tychonoff's Theorem
primitives.formalStatement: The product of any collection of compact topological spaces is compact in the product topology. This is equivalent to the axiom of choice. For finite products, the theorem follows without choice. It is one of the most important theorems in general topology.
primitives.computationalForm: For finite products: if X_1,...,X_n are compact, then X_1 x ... x X_n is compact (provable without AC). For infinite products: requires AC/Zorn's lemma. Application: closed bounded subsets of R^n are compact (Heine-Borel via Tychonoff).

dependencies.description: Theorem is about compactness of products
Product must have the product topology
Often applied in Hausdorff settings where compact = closed and bounded

compositionRules.yields: Compactness propagation: if each factor space is compact, the product is compact regardless of the number of factors
compositionRules.example: [0,1]^N (infinite product of unit intervals) is compact in product topology

applicabilityPatterns: Prove compactness of product spaces
Apply compactness arguments to infinite-dimensional settings
Establish existence results in functional analysis and topology
